mnemonics.title: Bresenham Decision Rule
mnemonics.content: P - for 'Pick': If P is Negative, pick Lower (Y stays same). If P is Positive, pick Upper (Y+1). Remember: NeGative = No Y increment, Positive = add to Y (Positive direction up).

mnemonics.title: DDA Steps
mnemonics.content: DDA = 'Determine Delta, Divide (by dx), Add increment': Calculate dy/dx (slope), Add to previous point, Round to nearest integer.

mnemonics.title: Bresenham vs DDA
mnemonics.content: Bresenham: 'Brilliantly Efficient, Requires Only Simple Math'. DDA: 'Dependable but Delayed (by floats)'.

acronyms.usage: Line drawing algorithm that uses differential equations and floating-point arithmetic

acronyms.expansion: Raster is 'Row of Pixels' - a grid display
acronyms.usage: Display technology where images are represented as a matrix of pixels

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a right triangle with the line as hypotenuse. If slope (rise/run) > 1, the triangle is tall and thin - step vertically. If slope < 1, it's wide and short - step horizontally.
Think of the decision parameter as measuring which side of the midpoint the ideal line passes through. Left of midpoint: stay low. Right of midpoint: go high.
Picture pixels as squares on graph paper. The line algorithm chooses which squares to fill to best approximate the straight line path.

analogies.analogy: Like walking from point A to B by counting steps: each step in X, estimate where Y should be (like asking 'how much should I move up based on the slope?').
Like deciding at each step whether to go straight or diagonally based on which keeps you closer to the ideal line - like a GPS recalculating at every intersection.
Like painting a straight line with a brush that can only make square bristle marks - you must choose which squares to paint to look like a line.
